World Commander — benchmark report (command arena + StarCraft II)

Natural-language command of agents in real time, under a latency budget. Phase-1 warm-up of the World Commander program.

Below is the arena in motion: coloured agents follow streamed natural-language orders while grey agents wander on their own clock. Press Play (or drag the slider) to watch what happens — then read on for what is measured.

Grid replay (you control the speed — drag the slider or press Play)

command 0 / 0

How to read the grid

  • Coloured agents — the ones you command (red, blue, green, yellow). The letter is the colour’s initial.
  • Grey agents — uncontrolled NPCs (marked “·”). They move one random step every tick on their own clock; you cannot command them. They are what makes a late command cost something.
  • Gold ring — the agent(s) the current command targets.
  • Fanned-out markers in one cell — several agents on the same square (the world has no collision rule), spread apart so none is hidden.
  • Axes — x is the column (0–7, left→right), y is the row (top→bottom); “north” decreases y. The title shows the command, whether it was grounded, and its latency.

What this is

The command arena is a minimal grid world that stress-tests one thing: can a language model turn streamed natural-language orders into the right moves fast enough to matter. Each step, one order is issued (e.g. “Move the red agent north”); the model reads the world state and replies with the moves. One order is trivial by design — the test is the stream, many and fast. Crucially the clock never pauses: the grey agents move on their own every step, so a late command concedes ground, exactly as a slow decision concedes to an opponent in a real game.

Results at a glance

90 commands · grounding 1.00 · deadline miss 0.71 · latency p50 815 / p95 1085 ms

Grounding is whether the moves were correct; deadline miss is whether they arrived within the tick budget. The model is accurate but often late — the definitions and the per-command-type breakdown below show where the time goes.

Run configuration

ModelQwen/Qwen3-14B-AWQ
Grid8 × 8
Controlled agents4 (colour-tagged: red, blue, green, yellow)
Uncontrolled agents (NPCs)4 (grey; move one random step each tick)
Tick budget500 ms — the per-command deadline
Commands in this run90
Seed0 (world + command stream are reproducible)

Metrics — what each number means

Grounding accuracy
Fraction of commands where the model’s move set exactly matches the ground-truth move set (right agents, right direction). Measures understanding, independent of speed.
Command-to-action latency
Wall-clock time (ms) from issuing the command to the model returning its action. Reported as mean, median (p50), and 95th percentile (p95).
Deadline miss rate
Fraction of commands whose latency exceeded the 500 ms tick budget. A late action is dropped — it simply does not happen — and the world ticks on regardless. This is the real-time penalty: correct but late still loses.

Why 500 ms? It is a real-time commander cadence — about two decisions per second, matching screenshot-driven VLM commanders for StarCraft II such as AVA (~2 Hz). It is deliberately one operating point, not a hard truth: a single deadline that everything passes (or fails) measures nothing, whereas 500 ms is discriminating here. The right deliverable is the whole deadline frontier below — performance vs budget — of which any single deadline is just one vertical slice.

Latency by command type

The arena issues three command forms: single-target, a positively named multi-agent subset (“the blue and green agents”), and the all-except group. Multi-agent orders name more agents, so the model emits more tokens and takes longer — the usual source of a high-latency cluster.

Command typecountmean latencydeadline miss
single-target
“Move the red agent north.”		28405 ms0.07
positive subset
“Move the blue and green agents north.”		28898 ms1.00
all-except group
“Every agent except the red one, move north.”		34857 ms1.00

The charts

Top — latency distribution. Histogram of per-command latency. The dashed red line is the 500 ms tick budget; everything to its right is a deadline miss. Dotted lines mark mean / p50 / p95. A split (bimodal) shape means two populations of commands — typically fast single-target vs slower multi-agent orders.
Bottom — latency over the stream. One dot per command in issue order; green = on time, red = deadline miss. Shows whether misses are scattered or clustered as the stream runs.

Deadline frontier

A deadline miss is simply latency > deadline, so this whole curve is computed post-hoc from one run — no extra inference. The right deadline is the world’s time-to-consequence (how long until inaction is punished), not a fixed tick, so the x-axis is shaded by operating regime. The 500 ms tick (dashed) is misleadingly harsh: it sits in the steep part of the curve. Slide right into the human-paced band (voice-issued command, consequences seconds away) and the miss rate collapses — at ~1 s the fast models miss essentially nothing. So the real question is not “can it beat a game clock” but at what time-to-consequence the agent stays viable, and how cheaply; the frontier shifts left with a smaller model, a terser schema, and a faster GPU.

Output-schema comparison

A language model writes its reply one output token at a time (a token is a short chunk of text, very roughly ¾ of a word), one forward pass each, so latency grows with the number of output tokens. An output schema is the format we require the reply to take (a system-prompt instruction plus a parser). Same task, fewer tokens out, lower latency. We compare three:

  • json (verbose baseline): [{"agent":"red","dir":"N"}] — explicit keys per move, most tokens.
  • pairs (terse): red:N blue:N — one agent:dir token per move.
  • grouped (terse, exploits structure): N: red blue — the direction once, then the agents moving it. Shortest, since every command shares one direction across its targets.
schemagroundingp50 latencymiss@500msmulti p50multi miss@500ms
json1.00815 ms0.71842 ms1.00
pairs1.00235 ms0.00248 ms0.00
grouped1.00205 ms0.00208 ms0.00

Same task, different reply formats. A terser schema emits fewer output tokens, so its frontier sits to the left. Largest effect on multi-agent commands. Watch grounding: a format the model follows less reliably trades accuracy for speed.

Model-size comparison

modelgroundingp50 latencymiss@500msmulti p50multi miss@500ms
0.6B0.54916 ms0.72925 ms1.00
1.7B0.41699 ms0.67868 ms0.93
4B1.00555 ms0.63567 ms0.88
8B1.00562 ms0.63580 ms0.88

Controlled back-to-back runs on a dedicated GPU 2. 4B is the sweet spot — top grounding and lowest latency; below 4B grounding collapses (0.41–0.54) and latency is actually worse (output is ~2.5 tokens for every model, so the small ones are not rambling — they just run slower kernels). 8B matches 4B for no gain; 14B-AWQ will not serve on one 2080 Ti. The frontier is hardware-dependent.

Command-stream load — how fast can commands arrive?

The arena's real test is the stream: commands arrive at some rate while one model serves them one at a time. Arriving faster than the model answers, they queue — and a command's deadline runs from when it arrived, not when the model reached it. We replay each model's recorded latencies through a single-server queue (arena/rate.py) at rising arrival rates — no extra GPU.

modelservice rate (1/mean latency)sustainable rate (≤10% unmet @2s)
4B2.00 Hz1.98 Hz
8B1.96 Hz1.98 Hz
0.6B1.34 Hz1.20 Hz
1.7B1.30 Hz0.88 Hz

Left: share of commands with no on-time action (late or dropped) vs arrival rate; each dotted line is a model's service rate, where backlog runs away. Right: p95 response (queue wait + service). Sustainable command rate tracks model speed (~2 Hz for 4B/8B). Shown at a generous 2 s deadline to isolate the load limit; at the arena's 500 ms deadline per-command latency already binds.

StarCraft II testbed — decision latency by model size

The same streaming-command core, scaled up to StarCraft II (LLM-PySC2, headless, our own vLLM on GPU 2). Each decision now carries the full game state plus unit/ability wiki — ~3000 input tokens vs ~200 in the arena — so a decision takes seconds, not milliseconds. This is the efficiency wall the program exists to attack.

modeldecisionsp50 latencyinput tokens (mean)miss@2smiss@5s
1.7B202741 ms30050.950.00
4B304520 ms32041.000.37
8B205344 ms30111.000.60
14B207494 ms31091.000.95

First real SC2 numbers (2s3z). Unlike the arena, latency here is monotone in model size — at ~3000-token context the prefill/compute scales with the model, so a smaller model is markedly faster (1.7B ≈ 2× faster than 8B). Every model misses a 2 s deadline; even the fastest needs several seconds. Win-rate is not yet meaningful (camera calibration is capped to reach the LLM, so centering is imperfect) and the clock is synchronous (the game waits for the model). Latency and token counts are valid measurements.

World Commander — Phase-1 command arena. Model: Qwen/Qwen3-14B-AWQ. Self-contained report (images and frames embedded); regenerate with scripts/visualize.py. Grounding/latency are real measurements; a late action is dropped under the unpausable clock.
